history: The client has a longstanding pattern of recurring conflicts and strained relationships, often marked by trust and boundary issues. Some relationships have involved individuals struggling with alcoholism, which may have contributed to instability and emotional distress. There is a history of aggressive behavior, possibly as a defensive response to feeling misunderstood or unsupported. These patterns have led to a sense of isolation and difficulty forming secure attachments.
unlovable_belief: I am bound to be abandoned
intermediate_belief: If I let people get close, they will eventually hurt or betray me, so I need to keep my guard up. If I show my true feelings, others will reject or abandon me. I must protect myself by not relying on anyone, even if it means being alone.
intermediate_belief_depression: If I am always alone, it means I am fundamentally unlovable and nothing will ever change, so there is no point in trying to connect with others.
coping_strategies: The client tends to withdraw and avoid social interactions, especially during emotionally charged times like holidays, to protect themselves from disappointment or hurt. There is a pattern of oscillating between seeking reassurance and isolating, and sometimes responding to perceived threats with aggression or confrontation. These strategies reinforce feelings of loneliness and sadness.
situation: Spending holidays alone and reflecting on feelings of sadness and loneliness.
auto_thought: Everyone else has someone to be with except me. I’ll always end up alone because I can’t trust anyone and nobody really wants me.
behavior: I stay at home by myself and avoid reaching out to anyone. I ruminate about past conflicts and reasons why relationships haven’t worked. I withdraw further and do not respond to messages or invitations.
